In practice, ImageWAM does not decode the target frame at inference time; instead, it conditions a flow-matching action expert on the KV caches produced by image-editing denoising, using them as a compact world-action context. ImageWAM outperforms standard VLA baselines and matching competitive WAMs without additional policy pretraining across different simulator and real-world experiments. It also reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis further shows that editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions, supporting image editing as an effective alternative to video-based world-action modeling.

AI.tldr: ImageWAM replaces expensive video generation in World Action Models with image editing priors, achieving better efficiency (1/6 FLOPs, 1/4 latency) and superior performance across sim and real-world robot tasks without extra policy pretraining.
AI.motivation: Video-based WAMs suffer from three coupled limitations: high inference cost from dense multi-frame tokens, wasted capacity on action-irrelevant details, and error accumulation in long-horizon predictions. The authors question whether video generation is truly necessary for world action modeling.
AI.method: ImageWAM repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. It uses the KV caches from image-editing denoising as a compact world-action context, conditioning a flow-matching action expert on them without decoding the target frame at inference time.
AI.result: Outperforms standard VLA baselines and competitive WAMs across sim and real-world experiments without additional policy pretraining. Reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis confirms editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions.
AI.conclusion: Image editing provides a better-matched prior than video generation for world-action modeling, serving as an effective, efficient alternative that focuses on action-relevant visual changes.

title: SIMBA: ABidirectional Retrieval Forward Simulation Framework for Modeling FY-4A GIIRS Hyperspectral Infrared Radiances Toward NWP Applications
summary: Hyperspectral infrared observations are an important data source for numerical weather prediction (NWP) because they provide rich information on the vertical structure of atmospheric temperature and humidity. However, most existing deep learning methods mainly focus on one-way retrieval from radiances to atmospheric profiles, while the reverse radiance simulation process and the consistency between atmospheric state space and radiance observation space are insufficiently considered. In this study, we propose SIMBA, a unified bidirectional retrieval-forward simulation framework for FY-4A GIIRS hyperspectral infrared radiance modeling toward NWP applications. The framework jointly performs atmospheric profile retrieval and radiance reconstruction, introduces a cycle-consistency constraint to strengthen the coupling between the two processes, and employs a bidirectional Mamba state-space module to capture long-range dependencies along pressure levels. Using collocated FY-4A GIIRS observations and ERA5 reanalysis data, the proposed method is evaluated for temperature retrieval, specific humidity retrieval, long-wave radiance reconstruction, and medium-wave radiance reconstruction. Experimental results show that SIMBA outperforms several representative deep learning baselines across both retrieval and reconstruction tasks, while ablation experiments confirm the contribution of the bidirectional design and cycle-consistency mechanism. These results demonstrate that the proposed framework is effective for joint atmospheric profile retrieval and hyperspectral infrared radiance modeling, and suggest potential for future Jacobian-related analysis and NWP-oriented extensions.

AI.tldr: SIMBA is a unified bidirectional retrieval-forward simulation framework for FY-4A GIIRS hyperspectral infrared radiance modeling, jointly performing atmospheric profile retrieval and radiance reconstruction with cycle-consistency constraints and bidirectional Mamba state-space modules, outperforming several deep learning baselines in both tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods mainly focus on one-way retrieval from radiances to atmospheric profiles, insufficiently considering the reverse radiance simulation process and the consistency between atmospheric state space and radiance observation space.
AI.method: Proposes SIMBA, a unified bidirectional framework that jointly performs atmospheric profile retrieval and radiance reconstruction, with a cycle-consistency constraint to strengthen coupling between the two processes, and a bidirectional Mamba state-space module to capture long-range dependencies along pressure levels.
AI.result: SIMBA outperforms several representative deep learning baselines across temperature retrieval, specific humidity retrieval, long-wave radiance reconstruction, and medium-wave radiance reconstruction tasks. Ablation experiments confirm the contribution of the bidirectional design and cycle-consistency mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is effective for joint atmospheric profile retrieval and hyperspectral infrared radiance modeling, and suggests potential for future Jacobian-related analysis and NWP-oriented extensions.

title: DiffusionVS: A Generative Framework for Robust Visual Servoing Based on Diffusion Policy
summary:
Visual servoing is a fundamental technique in robotic manipulation and navigation. Regression-based visual servoing frequently experiences trajectory jitter as a result of noise-sensitive single-step mappings and the accumulation of errors during distribution shifts. In contrast, Diffusion Policy maintains temporal consistency by predicting action sequences and improves robustness through implicit data augmentation.
  This paper presents a novel diffusion-based servoing method. Based on Diffusion Policy, the proposed approach uses normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners as input and generates camera velocity through conditional denoising. To overcome the generalization limitations of models trained on static datasets, an online training paradigm is adopted, continuously expanding the diversity of training data through interactive experience collection. This strategy substantially enhances both the performance and generalization capability of the model. Comprehensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving success rates of nearly 100\% in simulation and 93\% in physical experiments. Beyond the specific pipeline, we further validate the generality of the diffusion mechanism. Experiments show that existing visual servoing networks consistently achieve improved performance when integrated with our diffusion-based module. These results indicate that the proposed strategy possesses broad applicability and can enhance various visual servoing systems beyond the specific architecture presented here.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a diffusion-based visual servoing method that uses normalized image coordinates of tag corners as input and generates camera velocity through conditional denoising. An online training paradigm enhances generalization, achieving ~100% success in simulation and 93% in real-world experiments. The diffusion module also improves existing visual servoing networks.
AI.motivation: Regression-based visual servoing suffers from trajectory jitter due to noise-sensitive single-step mappings and error accumulation during distribution shifts, motivating a more robust and temporally consistent approach.
AI.method: The proposed method is based on Diffusion Policy, using normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners as input and generating camera velocity via conditional denoising. An online training paradigm continuously expands training data diversity through interactive experience collection.
AI.result: Achieves nearly 100% success in simulation and 93% in physical experiments. The diffusion-based module consistently improves performance when integrated with existing visual servoing networks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based servoing strategy possesses broad applicability and can enhance various visual servoing systems beyond the specific architecture presented.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of 3D Generative Diffusion Models across GPU Architectures
summary: Diffusion models have become essential for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, yet their deployment remains constrained by substantial GPU resource demands arising from hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and a highly heterogeneous kernel behavior. This paper performs a comprehensive performance analysis of the state-of-the-art medical diffusion model, Med-DDPM, across three generations of NVIDIA architectures to study kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix characteristics, memory system utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates. We show that training is overwhelmingly dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels, with inefficiencies arising from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, and limited Tensor Core utilization. Guided by these insights, we evaluate two architecture-aware optimizations TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout and demonstrate that they reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, all without degrading synthesis quality.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes Med-DDPM's performance across three NVIDIA GPU architectures, identifying cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels as key bottlenecks. Two optimizations (TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout) achieve up to 100x reductions in SM cycles and dynamic instructions, and improve Tensor Core utilization up to 9.98x without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are crucial for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, but their deployment is limited by high GPU resource demands from hundreds of U-Net evaluations and heterogeneous kernel behavior, motivating the need for performance analysis and optimization.
AI.method: Comprehensive performance analysis of Med-DDPM across three NVIDIA GPU generations, examining kernel-level runtime, instruction-mix, memory system, warp-level activities, and profiler priority scores. Then two architecture-aware optimizations are applied: TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last memory layout.
AI.result: TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, with no degradation in synthesis quality.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware optimizations can dramatically improve diffusion model efficiency on modern GPUs without sacrificing output quality, enabling more practical deployment of 3D MRI synthesis.

title: Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
summary: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

AI.tldr: This paper models multi-agent LLM deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden anchor (internal belief) that pulls its opinion. The anchor can be recovered from deliberation data, explains why agents can surpass initial consensus boundaries, and provides a test to distinguish anchor-driven models. Experiments across three model families show a spectrum of anchor influence.
AI.motivation: Existing models of multi-agent deliberation (like DeGroot and Friedkin-Johnsen) capture social/herd effects but ignore internal beliefs that pull individuals. The authors aim to model this hidden "anchor" effect to better explain why and how multi-agent LLM deliberation improves reasoning and accuracy.
AI.method: The authors model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden internal belief (anchor) that continuously pulls its opinion regardless of neighbors. They show this anchor can be recovered from deliberation traces and propose a test: checking whether recovered anchors predict held-out runs (generalize). Experiments are conducted on three open-weight model families.
AI.result: The anchor explains why an agent's confidence in the correct answer can exceed any agent's initial belief, escaping the convex hull of initial beliefs. The influence of anchors is equally strong across model families, but they differ in anchor position—only when the anchor sits far from initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull.
AI.conclusion: Anchor-driven deliberation is a spectrum, not binary. The proposed framework captures nuanced behavior that classical consensus models cannot, and the generalization test provides a simple way to identify truly anchor-driven deliberation.

title: Evaluation of EEG Foundation Models for Event-Based Burst-Suppression Detection in ICU
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure. Code available upon publication
summary: Burst suppression (BS) is a clinically relevant electroencephalographic (EEG) pattern used to monitor sedation depth and brain activity in critically ill patients, particularly during induced coma in Intensive Care Units (ICUs). Automatic burst detection remains challenging because BS patterns vary substantially between patients and annotated datasets are scarce. Recently, EEG Foundation Models (FMs) have shown promise across several downstream EEG applications, but their usefulness for BS detection remains unexplored. We present the first study to evaluate EEG FMs for burst detection in reduced-montage ICU EEG without patient-specific calibration. We compare REVE-base, LUNA-large and LuMamba-Tiny with an adaptive thresholding baseline and a task-specific EEGNet baseline. Additionally, we complement conventional EEG window-based classification with event-based burst detection evaluation. This helps assessing clinically whether burst episodes are correctly detected, reducing the impact of expected annotation variability. The best model, REVE-base, achieved the highest event-based F1-score ($0.868 \pm 0.167$) and reduced burst-per-minute error by 52.1% and 36.2% compared to EEGNet and adaptive thresholding respectively, supporting FMs for scalable EEG monitoring in ICU. Ablation experiments showed that full fine-tuning was the most effective adaptation strategy with respect to frozen-backbone training, two-step fine-tuning, and LoRA-based adaptation, improving event-based F1-score over frozen-backbone training by up to $+0.102$ for LUNA-large. With reduced labeled datasets, pretrained REVE-base outperformed random initialization by $+0.723$ event-based F1 points at 25% of the cohort, demonstrating the benefit of pretraining FM representations when adapted to burst detection with limited labeled data.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates EEG Foundation Models (REVE-base, LUNA-large, LuMamba-Tiny) for burst suppression detection in ICU EEG without patient-specific calibration. REVE-base achieved the best event-based F1-score (0.868) and reduced burst-per-minute error by 52.1% and 36.2% compared to EEGNet and adaptive thresholding. Full fine-tuning outperformed other adaptation strategies, and pretrained FMs significantly benefited limited labeled data scenarios.
AI.motivation: Burst suppression (BS) is clinically important for monitoring sedation depth in ICU patients, but automatic burst detection is challenging due to high inter-patient variability and scarce annotated datasets. EEG Foundation Models have shown promise in other EEG tasks but their utility for BS detection is unexplored.
AI.method: Evaluated three EEG FMs (REVE-base, LUNA-large, LuMamba-Tiny) against adaptive thresholding baseline and task-specific EEGNet baseline. Used both conventional window-based classification and event-based burst detection evaluation. Conducted ablation studies on adaptation strategies (full fine-tuning, frozen-backbone, two-step fine-tuning, LoRA) and data efficiency experiments.
AI.result: REVE-base achieved best event-based F1-score (0.868±0.167), reduced burst-per-minute error by 52.1% vs EEGNet and 36.2% vs adaptive thresholding. Full fine-tuning improved event-based F1 by up to +0.102 over frozen-backbone. With 25% labeled data, REVE-base outperformed random initialization by +0.723 F1 points.
AI.conclusion: EEG Foundation Models, particularly REVE-base, are effective for burst detection in reduced-montage ICU EEG without patient-specific calibration. Full fine-tuning is the best adaptation strategy. Pretrained FMs are especially beneficial when labeled data is limited, supporting scalable EEG monitoring in ICUs.

title: Site-Specific MIMO Channel Generation via Diffusion and Flow Matching: Fidelity, Efficiency, and Downstream Utility
summary: This paper explores the use of generative models to synthesize high-quality, site-specific multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channel data, addressing the high cost of the extensive measurement campaigns required to acquire real-world data for AI-native wireless networks. Two location-conditioned generative paradigms are compared: a conditional denoising diffusion implicit model (cDDIM), and a conditional flow matching model (cFMM). Both these models generate MIMO channel matrices conditioned on user coordinates, to preserve the spatial structure of the deployment site. The approaches are evaluated across three dimensions: statistical fidelity (including beam consistency and effective rank), generation efficiency, and utility in downstream tasks such as channel-state information compression and beam alignment. Results across diverse propagation scenarios (28 GHz and 3.5 GHz, both line-of-sight and non-line-of-sight) demonstrate that both models accurately capture site-specific characteristics, even when trained on scarce ground-truth data. Notably, cFMM achieves a quality comparable to cDDIM with roughly an order of magnitude less inference time. Augmenting scarce site-specific datasets with these synthetic channels yields hefty performance gains in downstream physical layer tasks compared to using scarce data alone or stochastic channels.

AI.tldr: This paper compares conditional diffusion (cDDIM) and flow matching (cFMM) generative models for synthesizing site-specific MIMO channel data. cFMM achieves comparable quality to cDDIM with ~10x faster inference, and augmenting scarce real data with synthetic channels significantly improves downstream physical layer tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world MIMO channel data acquisition through measurement campaigns is extremely costly and time-consuming, limiting the development of AI-native wireless networks that require large site-specific datasets.
AI.method: Two location-conditioned generative paradigms are compared: conditional denoising diffusion implicit model (cDDIM) and conditional flow matching model (cFMM), both generating MIMO channel matrices conditioned on user coordinates to preserve spatial structure.
AI.result: Both models accurately capture site-specific characteristics even with scarce training data. cFMM delivers comparable quality to cDDIM but with roughly an order of magnitude less inference time. Data augmentation with synthetic channels yields hefty performance gains in downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Generative models can effectively synthesize high-quality site-specific MIMO channel data, reducing reliance on costly measurement campaigns. cFMM is particularly promising due to its quality-efficiency trade-off, and synthetic data augmentation significantly boosts downstream physical layer task performance.

title: PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

AI.tldr: PerceptionDLM is a multimodal diffusion language model that achieves efficient parallel region perception by leveraging the parallel decoding nature of diffusion language models, enabling simultaneous captioning of multiple image regions through efficient prompting and structured attention masking, with competitive performance and substantial speed improvements over sequential approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which is inefficient for perception tasks requiring captioning of multiple regions; the goal is to enable parallel, efficient region perception.
AI.method: Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, the model uses efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, generating region descriptions in parallel at both sequence and token levels. A new ParaDLC-Bench dataset is constructed for evaluation.
AI.result: PerceptionDLM maintains competitive region captioning performance while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging diffusion language models, highlighting their potential for efficient parallel visual perception.

title: Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer: Scalable 3D CT Volume Generation
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/Fredy-Zhang/PRDiT
summary: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes with fine details remains challenging due to substantial computational demands and optimization difficulties inherent to existing generative models. In this paper, we propose the Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer (PRDiT), a scalable generative framework that synthesizes high-quality 3D medical volumes directly at voxel-level. PRDiT introduces a two-stage training architecture comprising 1) a local denoiser in the form of an MLP-based blind estimator operating on overlapping 3D patches to separate low-frequency structures efficiently, and 2) a global residual diffusion transformer employing memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across entire volumes. This coarse-to-fine modeling strategy simplifies optimization, enhances training stability, and effectively preserves subtle structures without the limitations of an autoencoder bottleneck. Extensive experiments conducted on the LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets demonstrate that PRDiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, such as HA-GAN, 3D LDM and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD and Wasserstein distance scores.

AI.tldr: PRDiT is a two-stage diffusion transformer framework for generating high-quality 3D CT volumes at voxel-level, using a local MLP denoiser for low-frequency structures and a global residual transformer for high-frequency details, outperforming SOTA models on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets.
AI.motivation: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes with fine details remains challenging due to high computational demands and optimization difficulties in existing generative models, especially the limitations imposed by autoencoder bottlenecks.
AI.method: PRDiT uses a two-stage coarse-to-fine architecture: (1) a local MLP-based blind denoiser operating on overlapping 3D patches to separate low-frequency structures, and (2) a global residual diffusion transformer with memory-efficient attention to model high-frequency residuals across the entire volume.
AI.result: On LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets, PRDiT outperforms HA-GAN, 3D LDM, and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD, and Wasserstein distance scores.
AI.conclusion: The coarse-to-fine modeling strategy simplifies optimization, enhances training stability, and preserves subtle structures without autoencoder bottleneck limitations, offering a scalable solution for voxel-level 3D medical volume generation.

title: Playful Agentic Robot Learning
summary: Current agentic robot systems can write executable Code-as-Policy programs, observe feedback, and revise behavior across multiple attempts, but they remain largely task-driven: reusable skills are acquired only after explicit instructions. We study Playful Agentic Robot Learning, where an embodied coding agent uses self-directed play as a continual skill-learning stage before downstream tasks arrive. We introduce RATs, Robotics Agent Teams designed for play-time skill acquisition. During play, RATs proposes novel yet learnable exploratory tasks, plans and executes robot-code policies, verifies intermediate progress, diagnoses failures, retries with dense, step-level feedback, and distills successful executions into a persistent code skill library. At test time, the agent reuses relevant skills from this frozen library to help solve new tasks. Experiments in LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces show that play-learned skills improve held-out downstream tasks over no-play and random-play baselines, with 20.6 and 17.0 percentage-point gains over CaP-Agent0 on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces, respectively. Moreover, the learned skills can be plugged into other inference-time Code-as-Policy agents by simply retrieving them into the context, improving RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points, respectively, without finetuning the underlying model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RATs (Robotics Agent Teams) that enable robot agents to learn reusable code skills through self-directed play before facing downstream tasks, achieving significant improvements on multiple benchmarks and real-world transfer without finetuning.
AI.motivation: Current robot agents focus on task-driven behavior and acquire reusable skills only through explicit instruction, lacking the ability to autonomously develop skills during self-directed play before downstream tasks arrive.
AI.method: RATs uses an embodied coding agent that plays by proposing novel exploratory tasks, planning/executing robot-code policies, verifying progress, diagnosing failures, retrying with step-level feedback, and distilling successful executions into a persistent code skill library.
AI.result: Achieved 20.6 and 17.0 percentage-point gains over CaP-Agent0 on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces respectively; learned skills improved RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points without finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Play-learned skills significantly improve downstream task performance and can be seamlessly transferred to other agents via context retrieval, demonstrating the value of self-directed play for skill acquisition in robotics.

title: Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning
summary: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

AI.tldr: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable probabilistic rules by projecting signals onto hierarchical abstraction lattices; the paper shows ILL produces constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables via maximum-entropy lifting, distinct from Bayesian networks.
AI.motivation: To establish a formal connection between ILL and probabilistic graphical models (PGMs), providing interpretability and theoretical grounding for the rules learned by ILL.
AI.method: ILL alternately projects a signal onto a partition lattice (inducing deterministic quotient variables) and lifts selected rules back; lifting uses an L2 uniformity principle akin to maximum entropy, and under Shannon entropy yields a log-linear factor graph indexed by learned abstractions.
AI.result: Probabilistic rules from ILL correspond to marginal constraints over quotient variables; general lifting yields all joint distributions satisfying constraints; special lifting (L2 uniformity) gives a maximum-ignorance reconstruction; Shannon-entropy lifting produces a log-linear factor graph.
AI.conclusion: ILL is best understood as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables, distinct from Bayesian networks, opening directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

title: DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision
summary: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

AI.tldr: DeXposure-Claw is a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system for decentralized finance that uses a graph time-series model, deterministic monitors, and confidence gates to reduce false alarms from LLM agents, evaluated via a regulator-aligned benchmark.
AI.motivation: Decentralized finance creates fast-moving, networked credit risks that are poorly handled by general-purpose LLM agents, which over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations lack regulator-aligned metrics to measure false alarms.
AI.method: DeXposure-Claw routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM (a graph time-series foundation model) forecasts exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios generate typed alerts and evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before emitting auditable tickets.
AI.result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system, with the DeXposure-Bench evaluation scoring tickets via regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and explicit false-intervention rate.
AI.conclusion: Structured evidence routing and confidence gating can effectively reduce false alarms in LLM-based decentralized finance supervision, with explicit regulator-aligned evaluation metrics.

title: Reliable ORIS-assisted FSO Communications via HARQ
summary: This paper studies a free-space optical (FSO) link assisted by an optical reconfigurable intelligent surface (ORIS) and enhanced by a hybrid automatic repeat request (HARQ) scheme. The ORIS creates a virtual line-of-sight path around obstacles, while HARQ recovers frames corrupted by turbulence, pointing jitter, and geometric loss through retransmission and combining. We first derive a tractable statistical model for the end-to-end transmitter-ORIS-receiver (Tx-ORIS-Rx) reflected channel by jointly accounting for atmospheric turbulence, ORIS-induced pointing errors, and geometric attenuation. Building on these results, we obtain closed-form outage probability (OP) expressions for HARQ with Chase combining (HARQ-CC) and analytical outage upper bounds for HARQ with incremental redundancy (HARQ-IR), valid for an arbitrary maximum number of transmission rounds. We further conduct a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) analysis that provides a thorough characterization of the outage behavior and reveals the diversity order of both schemes. In addition, we characterize the delay behavior of the truncated HARQ process through the mean number of transmission rounds and the conditional mean number of rounds given successful decoding. Finally, numerical and Monte Carlo results validate the proposed analysis and show that HARQ substantially improves ORIS-assisted FSO reliability, with HARQ-IR achieving lower outage and delay than HARQ-CC, even for a small number of retransmission rounds.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes an ORIS-assisted FSO link enhanced by HARQ, deriving statistical models, closed-form outage expressions for HARQ-CC and HARQ-IR, diversity order, and delay metrics, showing that HARQ significantly improves reliability.
AI.motivation: To improve the reliability of FSO links that suffer from turbulence, pointing jitter, and geometric loss, by combining ORIS (for virtual line-of-sight) with HARQ (for retransmission-based error recovery).
AI.method: Derives a tractable statistical model for the Tx-ORIS-Rx reflected channel including turbulence, pointing errors, and geometric loss; obtains closed-form outage probability for HARQ-CC and outage upper bounds for HARQ-IR; conducts high-SNR analysis for diversity order; characterizes delay via mean number of transmission rounds.
AI.result: Closed-form OP expressions for HARQ-CC, analytical upper bounds for HARQ-IR, diversity order characterization, and delay metrics. Numerical/Monte Carlo validation shows HARQ substantially improves reliability, with HARQ-IR outperforming HARQ-CC in both outage and delay.
AI.conclusion: HARQ significantly enhances ORIS-assisted FSO reliability; HARQ-IR achieves lower outage and delay than HARQ-CC even with few retransmission rounds.

title: Spatially Robust Near-Field SWIPT Using Pinching Antennas: Rate-Energy Tradeoff Bounds
summary: Pinching Waveguide Antennas (PWAs) offer significant potential for simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) by enabling precise near-field energy focusing. However, existing optimization frameworks are largely point-based (targeting a single coordinate for maximum gain), and thus highly sensitive to positioning errors and mobility, as near-field signals fluctuate significantly even over small spatial displacements. In this paper, we propose a spatially robust design framework based on discrete antenna selection optimized for service area (SA) coverage. Unlike point-based approaches, our model guarantees quality of service within predefined SAs for both information decoding (ID) and energy harvesting (EH) receivers, thereby improving robustness to user displacements. We formulate the problem as a non-convex binary quadratic program aimed at maximizing harvested energy within the EH SA subject to robust rate constraints in the ID SA. To characterize fundamental performance limits, we develop a semidefinite relaxation (SDR) framework that provides an upper bound on the achievable rate-energy (R-E) region. For the lower bound, we employ a low-complexity swap-based local search algorithm enforcing binary hardware constraints. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed coverage-oriented design yields a robust R-E tradeoff and maintains stable performance across service regions, highlighting the advantages of discrete antenna activation over point-based near-field optimization approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes a spatially robust antenna selection framework for Pinching Waveguide Antennas (PWAs) in SWIPT, using SA coverage instead of point-based optimization to improve robustness against positioning errors and mobility.
AI.motivation: Existing point-based optimization for PWAs in SWIPT is highly sensitive to positioning errors and mobility, as near-field signals fluctuate significantly over small spatial displacements.
AI.method: Formulates a non-convex binary quadratic program for antenna selection to maximize harvested energy in EH SA under robust rate constraints in ID SA. Uses SDR for upper bound (R-E region) and swap-based local search for lower bound.
AI.result: The coverage-oriented design yields a robust R-E tradeoff and stable performance across service regions, outperforming point-based near-field optimization.
AI.conclusion: Discrete antenna activation with SA coverage provides superior robustness over point-based approaches for near-field SWIPT systems.

title: Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Mix-QVLA is a mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models that uses task-evidence-aware sensitivity analysis to allocate bit-widths, reducing memory from 15.4GB to 4.1GB while maintaining 96.3% success rate (vs. 97.1% for BF16) with 1.52x speedup.
AI.motivation: VLA models face deployment challenges due to high memory and computational costs. Existing PTQ methods lack task-aware guidance for mixed-precision allocation and fail to capture dynamic sensitivity changes during task execution.
AI.method: Mix-QVLA computes gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations, compares full-precision vs. quantized maps via evidence-mass and attribution-distortion metrics, aggregates degradation via soft-bottleneck objective, and models time-dependent sensitivity for mixed-precision bit allocation under budget constraints.
AI.result: On OpenVLA-style policies, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB (73% reduction), retains 96.3% average success rate (vs. 97.1% for BF16), and achieves 1.52x inference speedup on LIBERO.
AI.conclusion: Mix-QVLA demonstrates that task-evidence-aware and time-dependent mixed-precision quantization significantly improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for VLA model deployment, making low-bit deployment practical.

title: Simulating Robotic Locomotion in Sand: Resistive Force Theory in an Open-Source Physics Engine
summary: Recent advancements in Resistive Force Theory (RFT) enable approximation of ground reaction forces for locomotion in sand without the computational expense of modeling interactions with individual grains. However, these tools have been absent in 3D physics engines commonly used for robot simulation. We explore if resistive force approximations are sufficient, when integrated with standard dynamics calculations, to provide a stable substrate for a freely walking robot. To determine this, we implement 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory (3D RFT) in a physics simulation engine, MuJoCo. We verify simulations in multiple scenarios to demonstrate that key trends due to end effector shape, speed, and loading are preserved. Our implementation predicts walking distance and foot sinkage of a 12-Degree of Freedom hexapod robot within 20\% of experiments in sand. While RFT has inherent approximations, the open source tool described here has potential to help develop new and improved robot designs to traverse granular media substrates.

AI.tldr: The paper integrates 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory (RFT) into the MuJoCo physics engine to simulate legged robot locomotion on sand, achieving predictions within 20% of experimental results for a hexapod robot's walking distance and foot sinkage.
AI.motivation: Resistive Force Theory approximations for sand locomotion exist but have not been integrated into 3D physics engines commonly used for robot simulation, limiting design and testing capabilities for robots traversing granular media.
AI.method: The authors implement 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory (3D RFT) in the MuJoCo physics simulation engine and validate it against multiple scenarios, testing effects of end effector shape, speed, and loading.
AI.result: The simulation predicts walking distance and foot sinkage of a 12-DOF hexapod robot within 20% of experimental sand locomotion data, preserving key trends related to shape, speed, and loading.
AI.conclusion: Despite inherent approximations in RFT, the open-source MuJoCo integration provides a useful tool for developing and improving robot designs for granular media traversal.

title: Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics
summary: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

AI.tldr: This paper explains why the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ grows, overshoots, and relaxes during AdamW training using a three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay). It introduces a spline displacement method to recover alignment forces from sparse checkpoints with ~92-94% accuracy, and observes data-dependent effects on λ's peak.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ during AdamW training, particularly its non-monotonic behavior (growth, overshoot, relaxation).
AI.method: Derives a three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay) of the squared weight norm from AdamW updates. Uses self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments and introduces a spline displacement method for real models without optimizer moments.
AI.result: Alignment force dominates the rise phase (88-94% of force budget). Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance. The spline displacement method recovers alignment force with ~92-94% accuracy, ~2x better than naive two-point baseline.
AI.conclusion: The three-force decomposition explains λ's trajectory during AdamW training. The spline displacement method enables analysis on real models. Data coherence affects λ's peak, suggesting a data-dependent component warranting further study.

title: LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

AI.tldr: LLMs fail to recognize their own knowledge limits on structured clinical data; verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous; attribution divergence analysis between LLM (Qwen 2.5 7B) and XGBoost reveals an inverse difficulty effect; combining few-shot examples with SHAP features reduces attribution disagreement (1.54→0.38) and boosts accuracy (49%→75.3%); a cross-model calibrator using divergence signals reduces calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080 without accessing model internals.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether large language models can recognize the limits of their own knowledge when applied to structured clinical data, specifically through the lens of epistemic uncertainty and cross-model attribution divergence.
AI.method: Compared Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a structured prediction task via attribution divergence analysis; applied few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence as super-additive interventions; developed a cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence signals to estimate LLM reliability.
AI.result: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of actual accuracy (49-75.3%); (2) Inverse difficulty effect: LLM accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct; (3) Few-shot + SHAP interventions reduce ADS from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy 49%→75.3%; (4) Cross-model calibrator reduces ECE from 0.254 to 0.080.
AI.conclusion: LLMs on structured data face a cold start problem regarding epistemic self-awareness; cross-model attribution divergence offers a promising path toward genuine uncertainty estimation without requiring model internals or repeated inference.

title: Hybrid TRP-UE Sensing for Enhanced Target Localization
summary: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) refers to the capability for the network to provide communications services whilst also being able to sense the environment in a scalable manner. One of the key functions of ISAC is the accurate localization of passive and mobile sensing targets. This paper introduces a novel hybrid TRP-UE sensing mechanism that improves network-based sensing performance. Evaluation results are provided using 3GPP-compliant ISAC channel models. The results demonstrate the significant benefit in complimenting TRP-based sensing with UE-assisted sensing in challenging propagation environments such as indoor factory.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a hybrid TRP-UE sensing mechanism for ISAC that improves network-based sensing performance, especially in challenging environments like indoor factories, demonstrated using 3GPP-compliant channel models.
AI.motivation: To improve accurate localization of passive and mobile sensing targets in ISAC networks, particularly in challenging propagation environments where traditional TRP-based sensing alone is insufficient.
AI.method: A novel hybrid TRP-UE sensing mechanism that combines transmission/reception point (TRP)-based sensing with user equipment (UE)-assisted sensing, evaluated using 3GPP-compliant ISAC channel models.
AI.result: The hybrid TRP-UE mechanism significantly improves network-based sensing performance compared to TRP-based sensing alone, particularly in challenging environments such as indoor factories.
AI.conclusion: Complementing TRP-based sensing with UE-assisted sensing provides substantial benefits for ISAC localization in difficult propagation environments.

title: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception
summary: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

AI.tldr: LIVE uses language instructions to dynamically guide vision encoders at inference time, producing task-specific embeddings without retraining, reducing hallucinations by 34 points on MMVP and outperforming much larger models on VQA.
AI.motivation: Vision foundation models are static feature extractors that require large downstream models for task adaptation; the authors propose a paradigm where language itself dynamically guides the vision encoder.
AI.method: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE) leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, using instructions to steer the encoder's attention to contextually relevant visual features.
AI.result: Reduces visual hallucinations by +34 points on MMVP, surpasses much larger vision-language models on VQA, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.
AI.conclusion: LIVE offers a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence without task-specific retraining, demonstrating controllable and generalizable representations.

title: Proprioceptive Invariant State Estimation for Humanoid Robots on Non-Inertial Ground
summary: This paper presents an invariant extended Kalman filtering (InEKF) approach for real-time state estimation of humanoid robots operating on non-inertial ground using only onboard proprioceptive sensing. The proposed approach estimates the robot's base position and velocity relative to the moving ground frame without requiring direct measurements of ground motion or externally mounted sensors. By exploiting kinematic constraints at the stance foot through foot-mounted IMUs, the filter accounts for ground-induced nonlinearities in the process and measurement models while remaining fully proprioceptive. The estimator is formulated to admit a right-invariant measurement model, enabling favorable error dynamics under large initial uncertainties. Observability analysis establishes conditions under which the robot's relative base position and velocity are observable with respect to the non-inertial ground frame. Experiments with the Digit humanoid robot standing and squatting atop a swaying and pitching ground showcase a 96% speedup in convergence rate and an 80% reduction in position estimate errors over existing InEKFs. Walking experiments on a uni-axially rotating ground achieve an average estimation error of less than 9 cm for an initial error of up to 1 m.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an Invariant Extended Kalman Filter (InEKF) approach for estimating humanoid robot state on moving ground using only onboard proprioceptive sensors, achieving faster convergence and significantly reduced estimation errors.
AI.motivation: To enable humanoid robots to estimate their state relative to non-inertial (moving) ground without external sensors or direct ground motion measurements, which is crucial for operation in dynamic environments like ships or vehicles.
AI.method: The proposed InEKF uses foot-mounted IMUs to exploit kinematic constraints at the stance foot, and adopts a right-invariant measurement model for favorable error dynamics under large initial uncertainties.
AI.result: 96% faster convergence rate and 80% reduction in position estimate errors over existing InEKFs on swaying/pitching ground; under 9 cm average estimation error on rotating ground with up to 1 m initial error.
AI.conclusion: The proposed proprioceptive InEKF effectively estimates humanoid robot states on non-inertial ground, offering practical benefits for real-world locomotion in dynamic environments.

title: Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms
summary: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as a novel sampling law for estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs), enabling EDAs to handle sparse optimization problems where most solution coefficients are zero, without requiring hand-crafted sparsity operators.
AI.motivation: Existing EDAs work well on continuous parameter spaces but cannot handle sparse problems (where most coefficients are zero). Current sparse black-box optimizers rely on hand-crafted sparsity operators, zeroing thresholds, and bi-level schemes—exactly the kind of design bias EDAs were created to avoid.
AI.method: The authors propose using multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. The ZIG model jointly represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and interactions between pattern and values via a latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions. They introduce amortized inversion-based estimators for the latent parameters, which are identifiable from observed samples.
AI.result: The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures. On the Lunar Lander benchmark, the ZIG-EDA converges faster and achieves higher final returns compared to a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers using only a small fraction of active parameters.
AI.conclusion: ZIG-EDA successfully extends EDAs to sparse optimization problems, eliminating the need for hand-crafted sparsity operators while jointly optimizing sparsity patterns and active values in a hierarchy-free manner.

title: REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk
summary: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a continuous formulation of phenotypic similarity for contrastive learning in vision-language alignment, replacing discrete group assignments with differentiable weighting functions derived from intra-modality embeddings. Applied to retinal fundus images and clinical narratives for Alzheimer's disease prediction, the method outperforms discrete group-based and standard baselines on UK Biobank data.
AI.motivation: Existing methods use discrete phenotypic groupings (hard cluster assignments) that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning, failing to capture the continuous spectrum of disease risk.
AI.method: The authors propose a continuous formulation where inter-subject similarity is modeled as a differentiable weighting function from intra-modality embedding similarities (retinal images and risk profiles). These weights define soft multi-positive relationships via a continuous aggregation operator, and a soft-target contrastive objective jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure end-to-end.
AI.result: On UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal (rather than a fixed grouping rule) provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

title: Max-Min Rate Fairness Optimization for Multi-User Pinching-Antenna NOMA Systems
summary: Pinching-antenna systems (PASs) can overcome signal blockage by repositioning dielectric radiating elements, called pinching antennas (PAs), along meter-scale waveguides to create line-of-sight links. Since each waveguide is driven by a single radio-frequency (RF) chain, non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) is well suited for PAS-based multi-user communications. This paper studies a PAS-enabled multi-user downlink NOMA system with multiple waveguides, each equipped with multiple PAs. The PA positions and base-station transmit precoding are jointly optimized to maximize the minimum user rate. The resulting problem is highly non-smooth and non-convex because of the rapidly oscillating coherent sums caused by inter-PA interference. To tackle this challenge, we propose a two-stage structured optimization framework. In the first stage, coarse PA-position and power-allocation optimization is performed using an interior-point algorithm while neglecting the PA channel phases, which gives solutions near the true optima. In the second stage, PA positions and transmit precoding are fine-tuned while accounting for the PA channel phase shifts. This stage first applies phase zeroing, where each PA is locally repositioned to align the corresponding channel phase toward zero and promote constructive coherent combining. It then uses an alternating procedure that iteratively performs forward-backward PA position refinement and successive-convex-approximation-based complex transmit precoding optimization until convergence, thereby reducing residual phase mismatch. Simulation results show that the proposed framework significantly outperforms heuristic optimization benchmarks with much lower computational time. They also demonstrate large gains over a comparable multiple-input multiple-output downlink NOMA system and reveal the impact of the number of PAs, users, and transmit power on system performance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a two-stage optimization framework for jointly optimizing pinching-antenna positions and transmit precoding in a PAS-enabled multi-user downlink NOMA system, achieving significant gains in minimum user rate and computational efficiency over benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Pinching-antenna systems can overcome signal blockage by repositioning dielectric radiating elements along waveguides. Since each waveguide uses a single RF chain, NOMA is well suited for PAS-based multi-user communications, but optimizing PA positions and precoding jointly is challenging due to non-smooth, non-convex coherent interference.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: Stage 1 uses interior-point optimization for coarse PA-position and power allocation while neglecting PA channel phases. Stage 2 fine-tunes via phase zeroing (local repositioning to align phases) and an alternating procedure of forward-backward position refinement and SCA-based complex precoding optimization.
AI.result: The proposed framework significantly outperforms heuristic optimization benchmarks with much lower computational time, shows large gains over comparable MIMO downlink NOMA systems, and reveals the impact of number of PAs, users, and transmit power.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage structured optimization effectively handles the non-convex PAS-NOMA problem by first coarse optimization then fine-tuning with phase alignment, yielding superior performance and efficiency.

title: Implicit Semantic-Aware Communication Based on Hypergraph Reasoning
comment: This work is accepted at IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: Semantic-aware communication has emerged as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication systems, shifting the fundamental goal from transmitting bit-level symbols to reliably recovering and understanding the semantic meaning of information. Previous studies have demonstrated that representing the semantic content of source messages as graph-based structures can significantly improve communication efficiency and the accuracy of semantic inference at the receiver. However, existing solutions typically employ graphs that capture only pairwise relationships, thereby neglecting higher-order implicit correlations commonly observed in real-world scenarios, such as group interactions, multi-entity associations, and complex relational contexts. This limitation reduces semantic expressiveness and makes semantic inference susceptible to ambiguity and performance degradation, particularly under noisy or corrupted channel conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework, HISR, which leverages hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships among semantic knowledge entities. In HISR, entities and their associated higher-order relations are mapped into dedicated semantic subspaces tailored to distinct relational contexts. This design not only disentangles diverse semantic interactions to mitigate the over-smoothing effects commonly found in traditional graph embedding methods but also enables robust semantic inference even when partial information loss occurs during transmission. Numerical results show that the proposed HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over the state-of-the-art benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HISR, a hypergraph-based semantic reasoning framework that captures higher-order relationships (beyond pairwise) among semantic entities, achieving up to 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based semantic communication methods only capture pairwise relationships, neglecting higher-order implicit correlations (e.g., group interactions, multi-entity associations), leading to reduced semantic expressiveness and performance degradation under noisy channel conditions.
AI.method: HISR uses hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships, maps entities and higher-order relations into dedicated semantic subspaces for distinct relational contexts, disentangling diverse semantic interactions and mitigating over-smoothing effects.
AI.result: HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over state-of-the-art benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hypergraph-based semantic reasoning effectively captures higher-order relational structures, improves robustness under partial information loss, and significantly outperforms traditional pairwise graph methods in semantic communication.

title: GB-LSR: A Fast Local Spectral Image Representation with a Single Global Bandwidth for Continuous Reconstruction and Super-Resolution
summary: We present GB-LSR (Global-Bandwidth Local Spectral Representation), a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction. The image domain is partitioned into non-overlapping square patches, each carrying coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable scalar bandwidth is shared globally across all patches and images, and reconstruction at any continuous coordinate is a fixed-size basis contraction whose cost is independent of image size. We study three bandwidth-handling variants: a trainable global scalar (main), a fixed global scalar, and a per-patch bandwidth field. On a standardized native-reconstruction benchmark across Kodak, Set14, and Urban100, the main variant outperforms matched-budget amortized LIIF / LTE / WIRE re-implementations by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS, while running at roughly one-quarter of the slowest baseline's inference cost. The single global scalar suffices empirically: per-patch adaptive-bandwidth alternatives do not improve over it on either a closed-form locality diagnostic or an end-to-end ablation. In a separate arbitrary-scale super-resolution (ASR) extension, GB-LSR achieves competitive PSNR-Y under a canonical-style SR protocol and runs 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN and 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4; within the same extension, a variant trained and evaluated without 4-corner local-ensemble averaging gives a 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory and negligible PSNR change, while additionally widening the RDN encoder from 64 to 96 channels gives a small positive PSNR shift with a 1.58x speedup and 31% lower peak memory. Native-reconstruction claims are scoped to the matched-budget amortized protocol, and ASR claims are scoped to a separate canonical-style SR protocol.

AI.tldr: GB-LSR is a fixed-grid local spectral representation using a single global bandwidth scalar for continuous image reconstruction, outperforming LIIF/LTE/WIRE baselines by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR at ~1/4 the inference cost, with competitive arbitrary-scale super-resolution results and significant speedups.
AI.motivation: To achieve efficient, high-quality continuous image reconstruction with a fixed computational cost independent of image size, using a spectral representation that avoids per-patch adaptive bandwidth complexity.
AI.method: Partitions the image into non-overlapping square patches, each carrying truncated Fourier basis coefficients predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable global scalar bandwidth is shared across all patches. Reconstruction at any continuous coordinate uses a fixed-size basis contraction.
AI.result: Outperforms amortized LIIF/LTE/WIRE by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS at ~1/4 inference cost. In ASR extension: 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN, 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4. Removing local-ensemble averaging gives 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory.
AI.conclusion: A single global bandwidth scalar is empirically sufficient; per-patch adaptive-bandwidth variants do not improve over it. GB-LSR achieves accuracy-efficiency Pareto improvements over existing amortized continuous representations.

title: A Categorial and Sheaf-Theoretic Semantics for Autonomic Component Ensembles
summary: The proliferation of large-scale, decentralized systems of autonomous agents, such as swarms of robots and networked cyber-physical systems, presents a formidable challenge to traditional formal methods. The Software Component Ensemble Language (SCEL) offers a formal model for such systems, but its operational semantics is not ideal for reasoning about global, structural, and emergent properties. This report proposes a new, multi-layered mathematical model for SCEL using category theory and sheaf theory. We argue that a society of robots described in SCEL can be formally modeled as a sheaf on a topological space, where components are points, ensembles are open sets, and distributed knowledge forms the sheaf's data. In this framework, computational processes like information sharing become equivalent to the sheaf-theoretic operation of "gluing" local data. System failures can then be understood and quantified as topological obstructions, measurable by sheaf cohomology. This approach transforms the verification of a complex distributed system into the analysis of the geometry of a mathematical object, providing deep, structural insights for the design of robust autonomic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a sheaf-theoretic model for SCEL (Software Component Ensemble Language) to represent decentralized multi-agent systems, where computational processes like information sharing are modeled as gluing operations, and system failures are analyzed via sheaf cohomology.
AI.motivation: Traditional formal methods struggle with verifying large-scale decentralized autonomous systems (swarms, cyber-physical networks). SCEL's existing operational semantics is insufficient for reasoning about global, structural, and emergent properties.
AI.method: The authors develop a multi-layered mathematical model using category theory and sheaf theory: components as points, ensembles as open sets, distributed knowledge as sheaf data. Information sharing is formalized as gluing local data, and failures as topological obstructions measurable by sheaf cohomology.
AI.result: A formal mapping from SCEL specifications to sheaf-theoretic structures is established, enabling the quantification of system failures through cohomological measurements.
AI.conclusion: This approach transforms verification of complex distributed systems into geometric analysis, providing structural insights for designing robust autonomic systems.

title: Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SPICE, a self-play reinforcement learning method that uses minimal human demonstration data (30 minutes) as regularization to train driving policies that are both effective and compatible with human conventions, achieving 2500x fewer human data requirements than imitation learning approaches.
AI.motivation: Self-play RL for driving policies learns alien, human-incompatible conventions; pure self-play lacks behavioral alignment with humans, and prior fixes (reward engineering, domain randomization) are brittle and labor-intensive.
AI.method: Treats a small amount of human demonstrations (30 min) as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward, rather than discarding or heavily relying on human data.
