AI.conclusion: The differentiable composite-approximation framework with joint calibration and current compensation is effective for field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling, outperforming pure polynomial, pure neural, and non-co-calibrated hybrid approaches.

title: Does Text Actually Help? Uncovering and Resolving Text Collapse in Multimodal Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multimodal time series forecasting, which pairs numerical sequences with domain-relevant textual reports, promises to inject world knowledge into forecasting pipelines. However, we uncover a critical failure mode in existing frameworks that we term text collapse: the text branch converges to a content-independent transformation, contributing negligible discriminative signal regardless of the input description. We argue that text collapse is a consequence of a fundamental asymmetry in time series forecasting: the numerical input is strongly autocorrelated with the output, making the numerical backbone inherently dominant, while the text branch, despite carrying complementary and often critical information, is insufficiently utilized, leading to its systematic underexploitation. To address this, we propose \textbf{REST-TS} (\textbf{R}esidual-\textbf{E}xclusive \textbf{S}upervision for \textbf{T}ext in \textbf{T}ime \textbf{S}eries), which turns the asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone produces its own independent numerical forecast, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual, the prediction gap that numbers cannot explain. Because no numerical pathway can reduce these losses, the text branch must extract genuine content from the input description. Evaluated across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently demonstrates greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks, providing strong empirical evidence that supervising the text branch on the residual compels it to extract genuine content from the input.

AI.tldr: Existing multimodal time series forecasting suffers from "text collapse" where text branches contribute negligible signal. REST-TS solves this by supervising the text branch exclusively on the residual gap that the numerical backbone cannot explain, forcing genuine text utilization and achieving SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a critical failure mode called "text collapse" where text branches in multimodal forecasting converge to content-independent transformations, failing to leverage the complementary textual information. This stems from an asymmetry: numerical inputs are strongly autocorrelated with outputs, dominating learning while text is underexploited.
AI.method: REST-TS (Residual-Exclusive Supervision for Text in Time Series) turns asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone independently forecasts, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual (the gap numbers cannot explain), forcing the text branch to extract genuine content.
AI.result: REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, with consistently greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks, empirically proving that residual-based supervision compels genuine content extraction from textual input.
AI.conclusion: Supervising the text branch exclusively on the residual prediction gap is an effective design principle that resolves text collapse in multimodal time series forecasting, forcing the model to truly utilize complementary textual information.

title: A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, Submitted to International Journal of Speech Technology
summary: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: This paper fine-tunes Transformer-based ASR models (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) on 870+ hours of Quranic recitation, achieving WER of 0.08 (EveryAyah) and 0.11 (combined), outperforming Citrinet baseline (0.163) while reducing training time from 140 to 40 hours.
AI.motivation: Existing ASR models have high Word Error Rates on user-recited Quranic verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus, hindering applications like memorization tools and search engines.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based ASR models (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) using self-supervised learning with masked audio input, fine-tuned on a filtered 870+ hour Quranic dataset with professional and user recitations. Includes ablation studies on feature extractors, label formats, training strategies, and clip durations.
AI.result: Best configuration achieves WER of 0.08 (EveryAyah) and 0.11 (combined setting), roughly 5-percentage-point improvement over Citrinet baseline (0.163), with training time reduced from 140 to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields best results; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 is the strongest representation.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific fine-tuning of Transformer ASR models significantly improves Quranic transcription accuracy. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

title: Neural Events: Discrete Asynchronous Autoencoders for Event-Based Vision
summary: Event cameras capture dynamic scenes with exceptional temporal fidelity by representing them as a continuous stream of microsecond resolution \textit{events}. Each individual event, however, only carries minimal semantic value, merely signaling a localized brightness change. To derive meaningful signals, downstream algorithms need to quickly integrate cues from a potentially massive torrent of low-information events. Current architectures, however, are easily overwhelmed, struggling to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics and maintaining a manageable data throughput. This paper proposes a framework to re-tokenize event streams into a small set of highly informative \textit{neural events}, each representing a local spatio-temporal context window with a discrete learnable code. Every time this code flips, a neural event is triggered, yielding a highly compressed data stream. We demonstrate that, across object detection and classification, networks trained on neural events are on par or surpass the performance of state-of-the-art approaches while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "neural events" - a compressed representation of event camera data that groups low-information raw events into discrete learnable codes representing spatio-temporal contexts, achieving comparable or better performance while reducing event rate by 2x.
AI.motivation: Event cameras produce massive streams of microsecond-resolution events that carry minimal semantic value individually. Current architectures struggle to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics with managing data throughput, as they are easily overwhelmed by the torrent of low-information events.
AI.method: A framework that re-tokenizes event streams into a small set of highly informative "neural events," where each neural event represents a local spatio-temporal context window encoded with a discrete learnable code. A neural event is triggered every time this code flips.
AI.result: Across object detection and classification tasks, networks trained on neural events match or surpass state-of-the-art approaches while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.
AI.conclusion: Neural events offer an effective compression strategy for event camera data, maintaining high performance with significantly reduced data throughput, addressing the core challenge of balancing temporal fidelity with manageable processing.

title: Bidirectional Tutoring for Developmental Motor Learning in Robots: Co-Developed Interaction Dynamics Support Stable Learning
summary: Infants are well known to develop their motor skills through dense interaction with caregivers. Although such social interaction is crucial for human development, motor-skill learning in robots is often treated as a unidirectional process in which robots passively receive demonstrations from tutors. This overlooks a key property of social interaction: it is inherently bidirectional, with tutor and learner dynamically adapting to each other. In such interactions, the robot's past experiences may function as prior constraints that shape the dynamics of their co-developed trajectories. We hypothesize that bidirectional tutoring allows such constraints to guide the formation of consistent behavioral patterns that preserve behavioral coherence and support generalization, whereas unidirectional interaction lacks such constraints and leads to broader, less consistent behavioral patterns. To examine this hypothesis, we conducted two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing an object manipulation task: one involving human-robot interaction and another employing an AI tutor interacting with the real robot through an adaptive intervention mechanism designed to examine whether similar effects would emerge under more controlled conditions. We implement the developmental learning framework using a free-energy-principle-based neural network extended with generative replay, which supports stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes. Across both settings, bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization, while the robot gradually required less tutor guidance. These results suggest that bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots.

AI.tldr: This paper explores bidirectional social interaction for robot motor learning, showing through human-robot and AI-tutor experiments that bidirectional tutoring (vs. unidirectional) leads to more consistent behaviors, better generalization, and reduced tutor guidance over time.
AI.motivation: Current robot motor-skill learning treats interaction as unidirectional (robot passively receives demonstrations), ignoring the bidirectional and adaptive nature of social interaction crucial in human development.
AI.method: Two experiments with a physical humanoid robot: (1) human-robot interaction, (2) AI tutor with adaptive intervention. A free-energy-principle-based neural network with generative replay was used for sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes.
AI.result: Bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behavioral patterns and stage-wise generalization, with robots gradually needing less tutor guidance compared to unidirectional settings.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, effectively scaffolds developmental motor learning in robots, aligning with how human infants learn through social interaction.

title: MortarBench: Evaluating Mortgage Loan Origination Agents
summary: Loan origination is the process by which a lender creates a new loan, from application and underwriting through approval and funding. This process serves a critical role in evaluating the eligibility and level of risk posed by an applicant. Recently, firms have begun using mortgage loan agents to augment human loan officers, despite a lack of any public benchmark. To fill this gap, we present MortarBench, a loan origination agent benchmark. MortarBench uses a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate examples with broad edge case coverage that match real-world distributions and questions. We find that state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) perform poorly, with closed-source models achieving at most 77.1\% exact match accuracy. We also discover systematic biases in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. Noting these weaknesses, we introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework. Our method increases accuracy to 80.5\% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.

AI.tldr: MortarBench is a new benchmark for loan origination agents using LLMs, revealing poor performance (max 77.1% accuracy), foreign-name bias, and proposing CRIT calibration framework to improve accuracy (80.5%) and reduce bias.
AI.motivation: Firms are using AI agents for loan origination but there is no public benchmark to evaluate their performance and safety.
AI.method: Created MortarBench via financial data synthesis pipeline; tested state-of-the-art LLMs; introduced CRIT confidence calibration framework.
AI.result: Closed-source LLMs achieve at most 77.1% exact match accuracy; systematic biases found against non-English names; CRIT raises accuracy to 80.5%.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are insufficient for loan origination; calibration methods like CRIT can help but significant gaps remain.

title: Benchmarking Agentic Review Systems
summary: A new class of agentic review systems are emerging as a remedy to the pressure placed on peer review systems by AI-assisted research, but it is unclear how they should be evaluated. We evaluate two open-source systems (OpenAIReview and coarse), one proprietary system (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline, across six LLMs spanning frontier and efficient models. First, we study whether AI reviews on ICLR/NeurIPS papers track with papers' quality as approximated by external signals such as citations and acceptance decisions. Every system performs above chance in pairwise accuracy, and the best is OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Second, to test whether systems can catch errors with known ground truth, we construct a perturbation benchmark that injects four categories of errors into papers across eight arXiv subject classes and measure detection recall. The strongest configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) catches 71.6% of injected errors, leaving substantial room for improvement. The union of detections across six models reaches 83.3% recall, suggesting different models detect different errors and better harness design can potentially increase performance. Beyond these benchmarks, we study a public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users. Votes on its comments skew positive at 1.44 to 1, and the most common complaints are about false positives and minor nitpicks. Together, by evaluating full review systems backed by state-of-the-art models on real research papers, we show that while AI reviews still have room for improvement, they can already track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive feedback from real users.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates AI-based peer review systems (OpenAIReview, coarse, Reviewer3) across six LLMs. The best system (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) achieves 83.0% pairwise accuracy tracking paper quality and 71.6% recall detecting injected errors. Real-user votes skew positive (1.44:1), but common complaints include false positives and nitpicks. The authors conclude AI reviews can track human judgments and catch errors, but still need improvement.
AI.motivation: AI-assisted research is overwhelming peer review systems, and new agentic review systems are emerging as a potential remedy, but there is no clear methodology for evaluating these systems.
AI.method: The authors evaluate three AI review systems (two open-source: OpenAIReview and coarse; one proprietary: Reviewer3) plus a zero-shot baseline across six LLMs. They assess (1) pairwise accuracy against paper quality signals (citations, acceptance decisions) on ICLR/NeurIPS papers, (2) detection recall using a perturbation benchmark with four error categories across eight arXiv subject classes, and (3) real-user feedback from a public deployment of OpenAIReview.
AI.result: Best pairwise accuracy: OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Best error detection recall: 71.6% (same configuration). Union detection across six models reaches 83.3% recall. Real-user votes skew positive (1.44:1 ratio), with main complaints about false positives and minor nitpicks.
AI.conclusion: AI review systems can track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive user feedback, but substantial room for improvement remains. Better model ensemble design could potentially boost performance.

title: OTCHA: Optimal Transport-driven Confidence-aware Latent Hub Alignment for Multi-View Medical Image Classification
summary: Multi-view imaging, such as mammography and chest radiography, is a standard component of clinical practice. However, medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can obscure diagnostically relevant findings. Many existing methods directly fuse per-view representations, allowing such irrelevant content to contaminate the fused embedding and reducing robustness under varying view configurations. We propose OTCHA, a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module based on optimal transport (OT) that refines patch tokens before fusion for multi-view classification. OTCHA introduces a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, we compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, and augment the OT formulation with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. The resulting transport plan provides token-wise matching confidence, which gates hub-mediated message passing and weights a novel optimal-transport-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement. Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/OTCHA.

AI.tldr: OTCHA is a confidence-aware optimal transport module that refines patch tokens before fusion in multi-view medical image classification, using learnable hub tokens and dustbins to discard irrelevant content and improve robustness across view configurations.
AI.motivation: Multi-view medical images often suffer from misregistration, view-specific artifacts, and irrelevant background cues that contaminate fused representations in existing methods, reducing robustness under varying view configurations.
AI.method: OTCHA introduces learnable latent hub tokens shared across views, computes OT plans between patch tokens and hub tokens with token-conditional dustbins for partial matching, and uses matching confidence to gate message passing and a novel OT-based alignment loss.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over competing baselines across three multi-view medical image datasets covering diverse anatomies and view configurations.
AI.conclusion: OTCHA effectively filters out irrelevant content before fusion, leading to more robust multi-view classification.

title: VOiLA: Vectorized Online Planning with Learned Diffusion Model for POMDP Agents
comment: Submitted to the 2026 International Symposium of Robotics Research (ISRR)
summary: Planning under uncertainty is an essential capability for autonomous robots. The Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) provides a powerful framework for such a capability. Although POMDP-based planning has advanced significantly, its application to real-world problems is often limited by the difficulty of obtaining faithful POMDP models. We present Vectorized Online planning wIth Learned diffusion model for POMDP Agents (VOiLA), a framework that learns task-agnostic POMDP models for online planning under uncertainty. VOiLA learns transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models and learns observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates. To enable efficient online planning, the diffusion samplers are distilled into compact feedforward generators and integrated with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP), an online POMDP planner designed to leverage GPU parallelization. Experimental results indicate the distillation strategy reduces sampling cost by up to nearly three orders of magnitude, making learned generative POMDP models practical for online planning. Evaluation of VOiLA on three benchmark problems indicate that VOiLA achieves equal or better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic while using less than 10% training data, and generalizes much better to unseen environment configurations. Physical robot evaluation indicates VOiLA uses the models learned using only simulated data and generates a policy that successfully accomplish the task in 10 of 10 runs.

AI.tldr: VOiLA is a framework that learns task-agnostic POMDP models via conditional diffusion models for online planning under uncertainty, distills them into compact generators for GPU-accelerated planning, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup and strong performance with less data.
AI.motivation: Real-world POMDP-based planning is limited by the difficulty of obtaining faithful POMDP models.
AI.method: VOiLA learns transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models, learns observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates, distills diffusion samplers into compact feedforward generators, and integrates with the Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP) for GPU parallelization.
AI.result: Distillation reduces sampling cost by up to nearly three orders of magnitude; VOiLA achieves equal/better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic with less than 10% training data; generalizes better to unseen environments; policy succeeds in 10/10 physical robot runs using only simulated training data.
AI.conclusion: VOiLA makes learned generative POMDP models practical for online planning, demonstrating strong data efficiency and sim-to-real transfer capability.

title: 3D-DLP: Self-Supervised 3D Object-Centric Scene Representation Learning
summary: We introduce 3D-DLP, a self-supervised object-centric representation learning model that decomposes scene-level RGB-D or voxel observations into a set of 3D latent particles. Building on the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework, each particle encodes disentangled attributes, including 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features, and represents a distinct entity in the scene. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps through an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. We demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable: by manipulating particle positions and decoding, we can generate novel scene configurations. Furthermore, we show that leveraging these compact 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance over baselines that either lack explicit 3D information or rely on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are available at https://eubooks3003.github.io/3d-dlp.

AI.tldr: 3D-DLP is a self-supervised model that learns 3D object-centric representations by decomposing RGB-D/voxel observations into latent particles with interpretable attributes (3D position, bounding box, appearance), enabling scene manipulation and improving downstream robotic manipulation.
AI.motivation: To learn interpretable, object-centric 3D representations from unlabeled data that can be used for scene understanding and control, avoiding the need for dense 3D inputs or explicit 3D information that lacks object structure.
AI.method: Extends Deep Latent Particles (DLP) to 3D: encodes RGB-D or voxel inputs into a set of latent particles, each with disentangled attributes (3D keypoint, bounding box, appearance). Trained end-to-end with a self-supervised reconstruction objective.
AI.result: Works on both simulated and real-world datasets. The latent space is interpretable and controllable (manipulating particle positions enables novel scene generation). Downstream robotic manipulation with 3D-DLP outperforms baselines lacking explicit 3D or object-centric structure.
AI.conclusion: 3D object-centric latent particles provide interpretable, compact, and useful representations for scene understanding and robotics, improving over dense or unstructured 3D approaches.

title: Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models
summary: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper defines four primitives for AI-blended architecture and two anti-patterns to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models, aiming to de-risk the integration of generative AI into traditional systems.
AI.motivation: Generative models pose risks when integrated into traditional systems, and the field lacks foundational frameworks to de-risk this incorporation safely.
AI.method: The authors define four architectural primitives for deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models and identify two overarching anti-patterns observed across industry.
AI.result: A framework of four primitives and two anti-patterns that enables successful integration of AI into traditional systems.
AI.conclusion: This framework de-risks AI integration and provides a foundation for generative model providers to build better interfaces in the future.

title: ViCoStream: Streaming VideoLLMs Can Run Beyond 100 FPS with Stage-Wise Coordinated Inference
summary: Streaming VideoLLMs must continuously process incoming video while maintaining low query latency, making both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness critical for real-time deployment. Existing methods largely focus on accelerating individual modules, such as visual encoding, token pruning, or KV-cache compression, but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. We formulate streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. Building on this formulation, we propose ViCoStream (Video Coordinated Streaming), a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework that combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. We further provide a systematic study of bottleneck migration, revealing how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off. Experiments with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks show that ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.

AI.tldr: ViCoStream proposes a coordinated streaming framework for VideoLLMs that achieves 134 FPS video throughput and under 50ms time-to-first-token on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-time streaming VideoLLMs require both high video-ingestion throughput and low query latency, but existing methods focus on accelerating individual modules without ensuring the overall system can sustain real-time streaming performance.
AI.method: Formulates streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline and proposes ViCoStream, which combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs.
AI.result: Achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct, maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.
AI.conclusion: Stage-wise coordinated streaming with bounded per-chunk costs is effective for real-time VideoLLM deployment, and systematic study of bottleneck migration reveals how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off.

title: Temporal Self-Imitation Learning
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation policies trained with reward shaping can still exploit dense rewards through inefficient interaction, while rare efficient behaviors may be forgotten during training. We argue that temporal efficiency itself provides a powerful and underutilized source of self-supervision for reinforcement learning. We introduce Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL), a reinforcement learning framework that mines temporally efficient successful trajectories generated during learning and converts them into reusable supervision for future policy improvement. TSIL progressively refines learning using configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets derived from fast successful trajectories, while preserving and replaying efficient behaviors through efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning. Across 15 distinct long-horizon manipulation tasks, TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training conditions. More broadly, our results suggest that the temporal structure of successful behavior itself provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning beyond manually engineered reward shaping alone.

AI.tldr: TSIL is a self-imitation RL framework that uses temporal efficiency of successful trajectories as self-supervision to improve long-horizon robot manipulation learning.
AI.motivation: Reward-shaped policies can exploit dense rewards via inefficient interactions, and rare efficient behaviors get forgotten during training. Temporal efficiency is an underutilized source of self-supervision.
AI.method: TSIL mines temporally efficient successful trajectories during learning, uses configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets from fast trajectories, and performs efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning to preserve and replay efficient behaviors.
AI.result: Across 15 long-horizon manipulation tasks, TSIL improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training.
AI.conclusion: Temporal structure of successful behavior provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for RL beyond manually engineered reward shaping alone.

title: Can In-Context Learning Support Intrinsic Curiosity?
summary: Effective machine learning depends not only on how we model data, but also on what data we choose to collect. While large sequence models have revolutionized data modeling, the problem of automated data selection, or "intrinsic curiosity", remains a significant challenge. Classic approaches incentivize exploration by rewarding an agent based on its "learning progress", which measures how much a newly acquired observation improves a world model's predictive ability. However, evaluating these rewards traditionally requires expensive inner loops of gradient descent updates within each trajectory, rendering them computationally impractical at scale. In this work, we investigate whether the emergent in-context learning (ICL) capabilities of sequence models can eliminate this bottleneck by serving as immediate, update-free world models. Specifically, we evaluate whether an exploration policy can be trained to maximize learning progress, using solely the prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations of an in-context learner. We first prove that in general Markov decision processes, this is in fact impossible in an unbiased way: the resulting intrinsic rewards either suffer from nuisance terms that bias their estimation of true learning progress, or they cannot be implemented using an in-context learner's prediction errors. Conversely, we prove a positive result for a broad subclass of non-temporal settings, encompassing active learning and Bayesian Experimental Design: here, ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to the true learning progress. We corroborate our theory with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, demonstrating that our ICL-driven framework successfully trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether in-context learning (ICL) in sequence models can replace expensive gradient-based inner loops for computing learning progress rewards in curiosity-driven exploration. The authors prove this is impossible in general MDPs but possible in non-temporal settings like active learning, with experiments confirming their theoretical results.
AI.motivation: Traditional curiosity/exploration methods require expensive inner-loop gradient updates to compute "learning progress" rewards, making them computationally impractical at scale. The authors aim to eliminate this bottleneck by leveraging emergent in-context learning capabilities of sequence models as update-free world models.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze whether ICL-derived prediction errors can serve as unbiased estimates of learning progress. They prove impossibility results for general MDPs and positive results for non-temporal settings. Experiments across continuous and symbolic environments validate the theory.
AI.result: In general MDPs, ICL-based intrinsic rewards suffer from bias or cannot be implemented. For non-temporal settings (active learning, Bayesian Experimental Design), ICL-derived rewards bound and asymptotically converge to true learning progress. Experiments show ICL-driven frameworks successfully train optimal exploration policies.
AI.conclusion: ICL can replace gradient-based learning progress estimation in non-temporal settings but not in general MDPs, providing theoretical grounding for when update-free curiosity is feasible.

title: Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers
summary: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

AI.tldr: This paper models expert-based QA forums as queuing systems, calculates system capacity for stable request handling, designs schedulers that achieve capacity, and explores how expert collaboration can increase capacity.
AI.motivation: To optimize request-answer processes in QA forums by employing knowledge workers with topic expertise, ensuring system stability while maximizing throughput.
AI.method: Models the QA forum as a queuing system with schedulers assigning requests by topic to experts based on their expertise levels; calculates system capacity and designs schedulers to achieve capacity.
AI.result: Identified system capacity for stable request handling, designed schedulers that achieve capacity, and showed that collaboration between experts can increase system capacity.
AI.conclusion: Expert-based QA forums can be optimized through proper scheduling and collaboration mechanisms to handle more requests while maintaining system stability.

title: PSCT-Net: Geometry-Aware Pediatric Skull CT Reconstruction via Differentiable Back-Projection and Attention-Guided Refinement
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

AI.tldr: PSCT-Net reconstructs 3D CT from bi-planar X-rays using differentiable back-projection, AGP-3D attention, and BiM-3D Mamba modules, evaluated on a new pediatric skull dataset (PedSkull-CT).
AI.motivation: To enable low-dose 3D CT reconstruction for pediatric craniofacial abnormalities from sparse bi-planar X-rays, reducing radiation risk while overcoming depth ambiguity and poor boundary delineation in existing methods.
AI.method: A geometry-aware framework with (1) differentiable back-projection for volumetric prior, (2) Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) for 2D-3D correspondences, and (3) Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) for efficient long-range volumetric modeling.
AI.result: Curated a private pediatric skull CT dataset (PedSkull-CT) with normal and pathological cases for evaluation, addressing the lack of pediatric craniofacial datasets.
AI.conclusion: PSCT-Net provides a geometry-aware, low-dose alternative for pediatric craniofacial CT reconstruction with explicit spatial modeling and efficient volumetric processing.

title: Data Standards for Humanoid Robotics: The Missing Infrastructure for Physical AI
summary: The scalability of humanoid robots will depend not only on models and hardware, but also on whether physical experience can accumulate across robots, tasks, organizations, and time. Drawing on the authors' work in developing ISO/WD 26264-1, Humanoid robot datasets -- Part 1: General requirements, within ISO/TC 299/WG 16, this article argues that data standards are becoming foundational infrastructure for Physical AI. We develop three insights. First, humanoid robot data is embodied interaction data, not a collection of isolated digital samples; a useful dataset must preserve the relationship among robot body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome. Second, its value depends on physical coherence: multimodal streams are reusable only when timing, coordinate frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization assumptions remain inspectable. Third, the main bottleneck is not only data scarcity, but non-cumulative data caused by high collection costs, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation. We argue that humanoid robot data standards address these bottlenecks by making embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable. A general standard should provide horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, and traceability, while capability-specific parts should define domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, human-robot interaction, cognition, and future humanoid capabilities. As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that data standards are foundational infrastructure for Physical AI, proposing ISO/WD 26264-1 for humanoid robot datasets to make embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable across robots, tasks, organizations, and time.
AI.motivation: The scalability of humanoid robots depends not only on models and hardware but on whether physical experience can accumulate across robots, tasks, organizations, and time. Current bottlenecks include high collection costs, data silos, inconsistent evaluation, and non-cumulative data.
AI.method: Drawing on the authors' work in developing ISO/WD 26264-1 within ISO/TC 299/WG 16, the paper develops three key insights about humanoid robot data: (1) it is embodied interaction data preserving relationships among body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome; (2) its value depends on physical coherence (timing, coordinate frames, calibration, kinematics, units, synchronization); (3) the main bottleneck is non-cumulative data. A two-tier standard is proposed: horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, traceability; and capability-specific parts for manipulation, locomotion, HRI, cognition, etc.
AI.result: Three insights about humanoid robot data: embodied interaction data requiring preserved relational structure; value depends on physical coherence; main bottleneck is non-cumulative data. Proposes a general data standard as horizontal infrastructure with capability-specific extensions.
AI.conclusion: As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction. Humanoid robot data standards address bottlenecks by making embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable.

title: Insulin4RL: Real-Time Insulin Management in the Intensive Care Unit for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

AI.tldr: Offline reinforcement learning for healthcare uses artificially discretized EHR data, which lacks realism. The authors introduce Insulin4RL, a MIMIC-IV-derived dataset of 375,000+ real-world insulin titration decisions with irregular timing across 12,209 ICU patients, along with baselines and evaluation protocols to advance ORL under realistic clinical conditions.
AI.motivation: Current offline RL in healthcare relies on temporally discretised EHR data, creating unrealistic representations and limiting generalisability. There is a need for a dataset with naturally irregular inputs and actions that reflect real clinical trajectories.
AI.method: The authors construct Insulin4RL from the MIMIC-IV database, extracting over 375,000 labelled clinical decisions involving insulin infusion titration from 12,209 ICU patients, preserving the natural irregularity of real-world clinical sampling.
AI.result: Baseline performance metrics are provided using model-free offline reinforcement learning, along with a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Insulin4RL enables future ORL research under realistic clinical assumptions, addressing a key limitation in healthcare RL evaluation.

title: Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework to address the optimization instability in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for LLM reasoning. ICT shifts focus from scalar uncertainty to token logit distributions using Jensen-Shannon divergence to identify critical tokens for selective updating, preventing both entropy collapse and entropy explosion.
AI.motivation: RLVR faces a fundamental dichotomy: uniform token updates cause entropy collapse (premature convergence to suboptimal strategies), while excessive entropy maximization causes entropy explosion (blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains).
AI.method: The ICT framework shifts optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to distributional properties of token logits, using JS divergence to identify tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points. Theoretical analysis uses Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy to prove that selective updates on these tokens regulate policy concentration.
AI.result: Updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 models (0.5B/1.5B/7B) yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58% (max 14.9%) over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven math, commonsense, and Olympiad benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The ICT framework effectively stabilizes RLVR training by selectively updating critical tokens, preventing both over-concentration (entropy collapse) and over-exploration (entropy explosion), leading to improved reasoning performance.

title: Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models
comment: Present at NeurIPS 2025 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

AI.tldr: MM-CBM extends Concept Bottleneck Models into CLIP using dual Concept Bottleneck Layers to align both image and text embeddings with interpretable features, enabling interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval with up to 51.26% accuracy improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing CBMs struggle with generalization beyond predefined classes and suffer from non-concept information leakage, where unintended predictive signals are exploited.
AI.method: Proposes Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) with dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) that align both image and text embeddings into interpretable features, extending CBMs into the CLIP framework.
AI.result: Achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks, and maintains high accuracy within ~5% of black-box performance.
AI.conclusion: MM-CBM successfully addresses generalization and information leakage issues in CBMs while offering greater interpretability with minimal accuracy trade-off.

title: Start Right, Arrive Right: Asynchronous Execution via Initial Noise Selection
comment: First version 19 pages, project site: https://paint-action-chunking.github.io
summary: Action chunking enables robot policies to produce temporally coherent behavior, but generating multi-step action sequences with flow-based policies incurs latency that is incompatible with real-time control. Under asynchronous execution, the robot continues executing the current chunk while the next one is generated, causing even minor delays to create inconsistencies at chunk boundaries. Existing methods address this problem by steering generation toward the already executed action prefix. We instead show that prefix consistency can be achieved by selecting an appropriate initial noise before generation begins, allowing the unmodified flow ODE to produce a coherent next chunk. This reframes asynchronous inference as a noise selection problem rather than a trajectory steering problem. We introduce \textbf{PAINT}, a training-free method that finds this noise via backward Euler inversion and constructs the final chunk through a repainting rule. In summary, \texttt{PAINT} requires no gradients, retraining, or policy modification; yet it improves execution consistency and task performance across \textit{12 simulated benchmarks} and \textit{6 real-world manipulation tasks} spanning single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments. Website: ~\href{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}{\texttt{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}}.

AI.tldr: PAINT is a training-free method that solves asynchronous action chunking inconsistency by selecting appropriate initial noise via backward Euler inversion before generation, rather than steering the trajectory mid-generation.
AI.motivation: Action chunking with flow-based policies incurs latency under asynchronous execution, causing inconsistencies at chunk boundaries. Existing steering methods are complex; the authors want a simpler solution.
AI.method: PAINT reframes asynchronous inference as a noise selection problem. It finds appropriate initial noise via backward Euler inversion before generation starts, then uses a repainting rule to construct the final chunk — no gradients, retraining, or policy modification needed.
AI.result: PAINT improves execution consistency and task performance across 12 simulated benchmarks and 6 real-world manipulation tasks (single-arm, bimanual, humanoid).
AI.conclusion: Prefix consistency can be achieved by selecting appropriate initial noise before generation, making the unmodified flow ODE produce coherent next chunks without trajectory steering.

title: Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

AI.tldr: Concept Flow Models (CFMs) replace flat bottleneck layers in Concept Bottleneck Models with a hierarchical decision tree to mitigate information leakage from spurious concept correlations, matching predictive performance while improving interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing CBMs suffer from information leakage when the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, allowing models to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations, which undermines interpretability.
AI.method: CFMs replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Internal nodes focus on localized subsets of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing prediction scope. The framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal.
AI.result: CFMs match flat CBMs in predictive performance while substantially reducing effective concept usage and mitigating information leakage.
AI.conclusion: CFMs yield stepwise decision flows enabling transparent, auditable reasoning with hierarchical class structures, improving interpretability without sacrificing accuracy.

title: AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA
summary: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

AI.tldr: AgentFinVQA is a multi-agent pipeline for financial chart QA that is auditable and deployable on-premise, achieving +7.68 pp improvement over zero-shot baselines with proprietary models and +4.84 pp with open-weights models, while providing confidence signals via a verifier component.
AI.motivation: Financial chart QA in regulated settings requires trustworthiness (knowing which answers to trust) and on-premise deployability (to avoid sending client data to external providers), but existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused, opaque, and assume proprietary API access.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline decomposing queries into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification steps, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample.
AI.result: +7.68 pp over zero-shot baseline with Gemini-3 Flash (71.24% vs 63.56%), +4.84 pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 locally. Verifier confidence signal achieves 68.2% vs 55.6% accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers.
AI.conclusion: Auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical; the open-weights system retains most accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. Error analysis identifies question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction error as key failure modes.

title: SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics
summary: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

AI.tldr: SurgVista is a surgical world model that tackles two key failures in robotic surgery prediction: spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse. It uses Deformation Consistency Regularization for physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics and Drift Adaptation Training to maintain visual quality over long rollouts. A new benchmark, SurgWorld-Bench, is introduced. SurgVista outperforms existing methods, especially over longer prediction horizons.
AI.motivation: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is difficult due to expensive expert demonstrations and safety risks of in vivo exploration. Existing surgical world models suffer from spatial interaction incoherence (inconsistent tissue deformation upon instrument contact) and temporal fidelity collapse (error accumulation causing visual degradation over time).
AI.method: Two training recipes: (1) Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence via latent contrastive learning. (2) Drift Adaptation Training perturbs conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics. Also introduces SurgWorld-Bench for rigorous evaluation with decoupled metrics.
AI.result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with performance gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.
AI.conclusion: SurgVista effectively mitigates both spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse in surgical world models, enabling more reliable long-horizon video prediction for autonomous surgical robotics.

title: EquiVLA: A General Framework for Rotationally Equivariant Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: Comment: First version 22 pages, project site: https://equivla.github.io/
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generalist robot manipulation, yet they lack geometric inductive biases: policies trained at specific orientations require substantially more data to generalize across rotational configurations. We present \textsc{EquiVLA}, the first general framework for end-to-end $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant VLA models, applicable to any architecture coupling a frozen vision-language backbone with a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. \textsc{EquiVLA} introduces \textsc{EquiPerceptor}, which produces approximately $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features; and \textsc{EquiActor}, an exactly $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. Together, they establish an approximate $\mathrm{SO}(2)$ equivariance chain from camera observations to predicted action sequences. Instantiated on GR00T~N1.5 and evaluated across four LIBERO suites, CALVIN ABCD$\to$D, and five real-robot tasks on Mobile ALOHA, \textsc{EquiVLA} achieves $92.6\%$ average success on LIBERO (vs. $78.1\%$ baseline), an average sequence length of $4.03$ on CALVIN (vs. $3.45$), and improves real-robot success from $54\%$ to $72\%$.

AI.tldr: EquiVLA is a framework that adds SO(2)-equivariance (rotational symmetry) to VLA robot models, using EquiPerceptor for equivariant vision features and EquiActor for an equivariant diffusion action head, achieving significant gains across LIBERO (92.6% vs 78.1%), CALVIN (4.03 vs 3.45 sequence length), and real-robot tasks (72% vs 54% success).
AI.motivation: VLA models lack geometric inductive biases for rotational generalization, requiring substantially more data to handle orientation variations.
AI.method: EquiVLA introduces EquiPerceptor (approximate SO(2)-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT) and EquiActor (exactly SO(2)-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head), forming an approximate equivariance chain from camera to actions.
AI.result: 92.6% average success on LIBERO (vs 78.1% baseline), 4.03 avg sequence length on CALVIN (vs 3.45), real-robot success improved from 54% to 72%.
AI.conclusion: EquiVLA is the first general framework for end-to-end SO(2)-equivariant VLA models, demonstrating substantial and consistent improvements across simulation and real-world robot manipulation tasks.

title: Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale
comment: 34 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957
summary: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

AI.tldr: Pretrained LayerNorm transformers sit near singular minima where Fisher information has dead directions. The inverse-scale direction of LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the centered activation covariance, computable from parameters alone with no forward pass. This direction deepens ~1000x from random init to trained checkpoint, predicts residual stream singular values, and classifies normalization type from parameters alone.
AI.motivation: To identify dead directions (where Fisher information vanishes) in pretrained transformers using only network parameters, without requiring forward/backward passes or eigendecompositions.
AI.method: The authors show that the inverse-scale direction γ^{-1}/‖γ^{-1}‖ of LayerNorm affine is an exact kernel of the post-final-norm centered activation covariance for any input. They test this on 14 pretrained transformers (9 LayerNorm, 5 RMSNorm; 160M-35B parameters).
AI.result: At random initialization, the predicted direction matches the bottom singular direction to 4 decimal places on all 9 LayerNorm models and is absent on all 5 RMSNorm models. The covariance eigenvalue deepens by ~1000x from initialization to trained checkpoint. Residual stream smallest singular values are preserved block-to-block on 13/14 models.
AI.conclusion: LayerNorm's inverse-scale direction provides a zero-cost readout of a dead direction in parameter space, enabling classification of normalization type from parameters alone and predicting singular structure without computation.

title: ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

AI.tldr: ORAgentBench is a benchmark evaluating LLM-based agents on end-to-end operations research tasks. The best agent passes only 35.51% of tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, with failures dominated by strategic weaknesses like missed rules and brittle formulations.
AI.motivation: Existing OR evaluations decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and fail to test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions, leaving unclear how capable LLM agents are at realistic OR work.
AI.method: The authors introduce ORAgentBench with 107 human-reviewed OR tasks across diverse scenarios. Each task is packaged in an isolated environment with natural-language briefs, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a submission schema. Agents write and run solution code, evaluated by hidden validators checking schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality.
AI.result: Experiments with 14 frontier agent-model configurations: best agent passes 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks. Many feasible submissions fall below quality threshold. Errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses (missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, insufficient improvement). OR-specific procedural skills increase feasibility but not pass rate or solution quality.
AI.conclusion: Progress in OR agents requires moving beyond generating plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

title: Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Linear Recurrent Unit (LRU) based super-resolution network enhanced with a Semantic Modulating Unit (SMU) that modulates LRU, categorizes spatial features, and enhances features via learned prototypes, achieving state-of-the-art performance with comparable computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: LRU shows promise for long-range dependencies but is limited for 2D vision tasks due to static parameterization and single-scan methods. The authors aim to adapt LRU for single-image super-resolution while balancing performance and efficiency.
AI.method: An LRU-based restoration network with a Semantic Modulating Unit (SMU) that performs LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototypes.
AI.result: The method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively bridges the gap between LRU's sequential strengths and 2D image restoration needs, offering a practical solution for single-image super-resolution.

title: TIDY: Thermal Infrared Image Denoising via Wavelet Domain Entropy and Directional Stripe Index
summary: Thermal infrared (TIR) imaging has been a popular choice for field robotics due to its robust perception capability under low light visual degradation, but it suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise that breaks downstream estimation. This noise is intensified indoors due to low thermal contrast and uniform temperature distributions, contributing to the relative lack of indoor TIR deployments. Existing TIR denoising methods exhibit a poor accuracy-efficiency tradeoff, either too slow for online deployment required in robotics or insufficiently robust to severe degradation, while typically being trained on synthetic noise. Addressing these problems, we propose TIDY, a lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser trained on real clean-noisy TIR data. By reformulating TIR denoising in the wavelet domain, TIDY explicitly disentangles noise from structural content, enabling targeted suppression with reduced spatial complexity, significantly improving inference speed over prior methods (~34Hz). TIDY introduces two new metrics, Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index, as complementary loss terms to explicitly suppress stochastic noise and stripe artifacts. Across severe indoor corruption and zero-shot settings, TIDY improves robustness and yields consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks including thermal inertial odometry and monocular depth estimation. Code and dataset is available at: https://github.com/williamrheeth/TIDY

AI.tldr: TIDY is a lightweight wavelet-domain TIR denoiser trained on real clean-noisy data, achieving ~34Hz inference speed, using Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index losses for targeted noise suppression, showing robust performance in indoor robotics tasks.
AI.motivation: TIR imaging suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise, especially indoors due to low thermal contrast, and existing denoising methods have poor accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs or are trained on synthetic noise, limiting real-time robotics deployment.
AI.method: TIDY reformulates TIR denoising in the wavelet domain to explicitly disentangle noise from structural content, reduces spatial complexity, and introduces Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index as complementary loss terms for targeted noise suppression. It is trained on real clean-noisy TIR data.
AI.result: TIDY achieves ~34Hz inference speed, outperforms prior methods in robustness across severe indoor corruption and zero-shot settings, and yields consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks (thermal inertial odometry, monocular depth estimation).
AI.conclusion: Wavelet-domain denoising with real-data training and specialized loss terms enables efficient, robust TIR denoising suitable for online robotics deployment, addressing the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff in existing methods.

title: Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning
summary: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.
